skill.name: n8n
skill.description: n8n workflow automation patterns and API integration. This skill should be used when creating n8n workflows, using webhooks, managing workflows via REST API, or integrating n8n with MCP servers. Covers workflow JSON structure, node patterns, and automation best practices.
skill.summary: n8n workflow automation patterns and API integration. This skill should be used when creating n8n wo...
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static findings were mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences, n8n node type names, placeholder files, and example URLs. The confirmed risk is legitimate but sensitive use of curl commands with an n8n API key to list, create, update, activate, deactivate, and inspect workflow executions. No prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, obfuscation, or unauthorized credential harvesting was found.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Administration Through API Key Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes curl examples that use X-N8N-API-KEY to list, create, update, activate, deactivate, and inspect workflows. This is legitimate n8n administration guidance, but misuse could modify automations or expose execution data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documentation for n8n REST API administration and include an API key header. They target localhost, so the risk is sensitive operational capability rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External API Request Pattern
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates an HTTP Request node that posts workflow data to an external API using n8n credentials. This is expected for automation workflows, but users must verify destination URLs and data minimization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example shows a generic external URL and credential-backed request. It is a normal n8n pattern, but it can move workflow data outside the n8n instance when adapted.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts correspond to ordinary n8n terminology, Markdown placeholder text, and node type strings. No cryptographic implementation or weak hashing use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context shows these alerts are caused by prose and example names, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or password storage behavior appears in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts come from JSON examples, inline n8n expressions, and Markdown code fences. The only actionable command examples are documented curl calls for a local n8n API.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation and contains no executable script entry point. The static scanner matched formatting and inline examples across many non-shell sections.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Mutating REST Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented curl commands include POST and PUT operations against the n8n API. Users should require explicit confirmation before running generated commands that create, update, activate, or deactivate workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mutating REST calls are clearly present and use an API key header. They are not malicious by themselves, but they can alter running automation state.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable help creating n8n workflows without memorizing node JSON and API details. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for webhooks, REST API use, expressions, and MCP integration.
actual_capabilities: Explains the standard JSON structure for n8n workflows, nodes, connections, and settings.
Provides patterns for webhook-triggered workflows and webhook responses.
Shows how to use HTTP Request, IF, Split In Batches, and related n8n node types.
Documents n8n REST API commands for listing, creating, updating, activating, and inspecting workflows.
Summarizes common n8n expression syntax for reading item data and node outputs.
Describes how n8n workflows can be exposed through MCP server settings.
limitations: It does not include full official n8n API reference coverage.
The reference and asset files are placeholders, not production templates.
API examples require a running n8n instance and a valid API key.
It does not validate generated workflow JSON against a live n8n server.

use_cases.title: Create a webhook automation
use_cases.description: Generate a webhook workflow that receives HTTP input, processes the payload, and returns a controlled response.

use_cases.title: Manage workflows through the API
use_cases.description: Draft safe n8n REST API commands for listing, updating, activating, or deactivating workflows.

use_cases.title: Plan MCP-enabled automations
use_cases.description: Design workflows that can be exposed as MCP tools for AI-assisted operations.

prompt_templates.title: Start a webhook workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an n8n webhook workflow that accepts a POST request, reads the JSON body, and returns a simple JSON response.

prompt_templates.title: Add routing logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an n8n workflow that receives webhook data, checks a status field, and routes successful and failed items to different branches.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare API management commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Write n8n REST API commands to list workflows, inspect one workflow, and activate it. Use placeholders for host, workflow ID, and API key.

prompt_templates.title: Design an MCP automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an n8n workflow that can be exposed as an MCP tool. Include trigger design, node flow, credentials, error handling, and testing steps.

output_examples.input: Create a webhook workflow for receiving customer support events.
output_examples.output: A workflow plan with a webhook trigger, payload validation, conditional routing by event type, and a response node for consistent API replies.

output_examples.input: Help me manage an existing workflow through the n8n API.
output_examples.output: A short command checklist using placeholders for the host, workflow ID, and API key, with separate read-only and mutating actions.

output_examples.input: Explain how to reference data from another node.
output_examples.output: A concise explanation of n8n expressions for current item data, nested fields, first input item, all input items, and named node output.

best_practices: Use n8n credentials instead of hardcoding secrets in workflow JSON.
Test webhook workflows with development paths before activating production endpoints.
Review every generated mutating API command before running it against a live n8n instance.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys into prompts or generated workflow examples.
Do not activate workflows before validating triggers, credentials, and error handling.
Do not send full execution data to external APIs unless the data has been reviewed.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create and manage n8n workflow patterns, including webhooks, API requests, branching, batching, and MCP exposure.

faq.question: Does it require access to my n8n instance?
faq.answer: The skill itself is documentation. API examples only work when you provide a host, workflow ID, and valid n8n API key.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready workflows?
faq.answer: It can draft useful workflow structures, but you should test and validate them in n8n before production use.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with secrets?
faq.answer: Use placeholders in prompts and store real secrets in n8n credentials. Do not share real API keys in chat.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It documents commands that can modify n8n workflows with an API key. That is legitimate but requires careful review.
